summary:
The Later Lê Dynasty (1428–1789 AD) was Vietnam's longest-ruling dynasty, originally established by Lê Lợi after his successful guerrilla campaign expelled the Ming Chinese occupation — one of the most significant acts of Vietnamese national resistance in history. Lê Lợi's Lam Sơn uprising (1418–1427) united Vietnamese resistance against the brutal Ming occupation that had attempted to assimilate Vietnam into China, imposing Chinese language, dress, and customs. After victory, Lê Lợi took the title Emperor Lê Thái Tổ and established a Chinese-influenced Confucian state centered at Thăng Long (modern Hanoi), with a centralized bureaucracy, civil service examinations, and codified law — the Hồng Đức Code — that represented Vietnam's most sophisticated legal system.

The dynasty's later centuries were marked by the fragmentation of actual power. From 1527 onward, the Lê emperors became increasingly nominal rulers, while real power was exercised first by the Mạc usurpers, then by the rival [[event:3304|Trịnh lords]] in the north and [[event:3303|Nguyễn lords]] in the south — creating an effective partition of Vietnam that lasted from 1600 to 1775. The Lê emperors were preserved as symbolic rulers despite having no real power — a Vietnamese parallel to Japan's relationship between emperors and shogunates. The [[event:2517|Tây Sơn Rebellion]] (1771–1802) finally ended the dynasty, sweeping away both the Lê emperors and the Trịnh-Nguyễn division before itself being overthrown by the [[event:3249|Nguyễn dynasty]].
